02

Projects

STAR @ UC Berkeley · Liquid Propulsion

DIABLO

Liquid Engine 3 · Ethanol / LOX · Pintle Injector

DIABLO was STAR's third liquid engine. I led design for manufacturability for aerostructures and propulsion with the goal of pulling machining in-house to reduce costs. My focus was teaching DFM to my peers so the team would build a structurally sound vehicle; I led DFM reviews and made executive decisions early and throughout the machining processes, redesigning parts to match what our student shop could hold and designed unconventional setups. Materials spanned mild steel, phenolic, graphite, aluminum, and stainless.

I was also the lead machinist for the combustion chamber and feed system. The ablative chamber required sequencing five materials in tight tolerances: mild steel outer sleeve, phenolic liner, phenolic-to-graphite donut, graphite throat, aluminum nozzle. I also machined the aluminum integrated ethanol tank and the stainless pintle injector. All the hardware came together.

I also undertook structural integrity on the combustion chamber. After multiple failed hotfire attempts, I ran FEA on the chamber and redesigned it for higher safety factors without compromising DFM or part-to-assembly standards.

Ultimately, the engine didn't fire. The failure traced back to the feed system design where these issues weren't caught until late in integration.

Propellants

Ethanol / Liquid Oxygen

Injector Type

Stainless Steel Pintle

Chamber

Ablative · Mild Steel · Phenolic · Graphite · Aluminum Nozzle

STAR @ UC Berkeley · Solid Propulsion

Gojira

O-5500 Solid Motor · Mach 1.43 · 20,000 ft

Gojira was originally designed as STAR's solid demo vehicle for LE3, to validate the airframe before integrating the liquid engine. When DIABLO's hotfire attempts stalled, the mission shifted. Gojira became a standalone recovery and payload vehicle to validate Mach and apogee simulations on the O-5500 motor.

It flew. 20,000 feet, Mach 1.43. The simulations held.

Motor

O-5500 Solid

Apogee

~20,000 ft

Max Velocity

Mach 1.43

Mission

Recovery & payload · Mach and apogee simulation validation

UC Berkeley Physics · Atomic Physics Lab · In Progress

Optical Pumping

Zeeman Splitting of Rubidium via Helmholtz Coil Magnetic Field Control

This experiment measures the Zeeman splitting of rubidium energy levels using optical pumping. A controlled magnetic field is applied via a Helmholtz coil pair, and the hyperfine transitions are driven and detected optically. My partner and I designed the apparatus, characterized the coils, and built the magnet driver and field modulation circuit from previous experiments.

We theoretically verified that our setup produces correct Zeeman splitting of rubidium, confirming the experiment will yield valid results when complete.

Method

Optical Pumping · Lock-in Detection · Field Modulation

Apparatus

Helmholtz Coils (R = 27.5 cm, 135 turns) · Rubidium discharge lamp · Photodetector

Electronics

Custom magnet driver & field modulation circuit, designed and hand-wired

Result

Theoretical verification of correct Zeeman splitting confirmed
